narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male applicant with a solid background, we understand how disappointing it must be to receive this law school admission decision, and we're here to walk you through the reasoning behind it in plain terms. The admissions model looks at patterns from thousands of past students to predict whether someone is likely to pass the bar exam on their first try, and unfortunately, it estimated a 48.3% chance that you would not pass—high enough to tip the scales toward rejection to protect the school's outcomes. Your profile includes a Year 1 school grade decile of 2, a Year 3 decile of 2, low family income, an LSAT score of 33, an undergraduate GPA of 2.20, and plans to attend full-time, which is the norm for most applicants. The biggest factor pulling the prediction toward bar failure was your Year 3 grade decile, as being in the lower ranks there strongly signaled higher risk compared to students who typically rank much higher around the middle of their classes. On a brighter note, your LSAT score pushed meaningfully in the favorable direction, performing better than it might for some but still below the average of 37 that helps many succeed. Your low family income also helped your case somewhat, as the model sees students from similar backgrounds sometimes overcoming odds to pass. Your Year 1 grade decile added some concern by leaning toward failure risk, while your undergraduate GPA had a smaller nudge in that direction, and your full-time status offered a slight positive boost since that's the common path. Overall, compared to typical admitted students who boast higher deciles around 5-6, stronger GPAs near 3.2, and LSATs closer to 37, your academics stood out as the key gap making the model cautious. This isn't a judgment on your potential—it's just one data-driven view based on historical patterns. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost that score, strengthening your GPA through post-bac courses or highlighting upward trends in later grades, and gathering strong letters that speak to your resilience from a low-income background. Many students in your position have improved these areas and succeeded elsewhere, so we encourage you to explore other programs or prep strategies tailored to bar success. You've got this—keep pushing.
